sentences.text: Yes.
So the people came into the ghetto.
They were laying in the streets.
No housing, no nothing.
But--
It was a-- the whole city was going to the ghetto.
It-- it was far when you had the bundles and everything.
I had a-- my brother had, already, the kid.
So they had to take a-- a bathtub for him.
And-- and who was-- I was the only one who could carry everything.
You-- you got in over there, and German was-- was-- or Ukrainian was standing with-- with a rifle.
And-- and-- and I didn't do what he wanted me to do, something.
So he went after me with the-- with the rifle.
But I was better than him for these things.
And I got in.
We get in.
Everybody's was laying-- we came into the ghetto, was laying like this, on the street.
It was no place for nobody.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But the people who were living there, and they got-- got took out to-- to-- to-- to [INAUDIBLE].
You know, to the place where they used to shooting people.
And the-- the people who came in, they didn't have no strength to move.
And everybody that was laying.
And this was women and children and-- and kids.
Was-- it was bad.
I had some tools with me.
So I went in the houses that was locked.
Because the people were thinking they come back.
And I had the tools, so I opened up a couple of the apartments.
And I let's in these people.
Over there, there was the tea was staying on-- on-- on the table.
And-- and-- and the food and everything.
They took them out from the houses.
So we got in, you know?
With the-- but the whole-- from my apartment, I took the whole people with me, took them in.
And we had at least, at night, where to sleep.
And-- and me, I slept a-- a little, because you have to eat.
Nothing to eat, and we cannot go out of the ghetto.
Because the Jewish, they used the Jews.
They used to-- they came-- they came to-- to-- to [INAUDIBLE], you know, that means like this for death.
But I was-- I-- I wasn't normal after all these things.
[LAUGHS] Tomorrow morning, I got up.
I went out to buy food.
Because the-- there was a lot-- [INAUDIBLE] without it.
Without this thing.
I used-- you-- you have to have what everyone had.
A Magen David.
Yellow.
You had to--
You had to wear two, you know, one in the back and one in the front.
So-- so but I know I don't look like Jewish.
You could get out.
But you didn't have no place to go.
You didn't have no place to buy.
And you didn't have nothing.
So you had to go out and take your chances.
I know some [?goy?], some Gentiles who used to have this business.
You know, before.
We used to have-- the groceries, we used to buy from them.
You know, for the food, you know, for the groceries.
So I figured, what do I got to lose?
I'll die here anyway.
So I went out without a Magen David, you know?
And from-- from the ghetto, you know, nobody wanted to keep you there.
You know?
[LAUGHS] Go.
Do what you want to.
So I went out.
I took off the-- I took off the things, start going.
And-- and a Jew wasn't allowed to go on the-- on the sidewalk.
He had to go in the streets.
So I took it off and went on the sidewalk.
And I went over there, where I had to go.
I came over there.
He says, I haven't got nothing to sell you.
I said, what do you mean, you haven't got nothing to sell me?
I haven't got nothing.
Sell me something.
I have to come back.
He says, the only thing I got, I got strawberries.
Can you imagine, strawberries?
And we were hungry.
I haven't-- here I haven't got a-- a-- a-- where to put it.
[LAUGHS] [BACKGROUND NOISE]
So I took it.
He gave me a basket like this, you know-- you know, keeping it bigger.
I took strawberries.
And I had the nerve to go with these strawberries in the street, on the sidewalk.
[LAUGHTER] And did you succeed in bringing it back into the ghetto?
Sure, I succeeded.
Well, and you know what?
I was rich in the ghetto.
[INAUDIBLE] stupid.
[LAUGHTER] Yeah.
I used to go out every day.
Then I knew everybody.
And you know, the police, because I used to-- you have to, you know, anything you bring in, they knew it.
And [NON-ENGLISH], but everything, every day, I had the same thing.
Every day, something.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then when it came the-- the thing to go out of the ghettos, they knew me that I was a soldier.
I was you know, a bigger soldier.
And they need somebody to instruct the guys how to handle a-- a gun, how to handle a-- anything.
And they knew me from before.
So they called me right away.
And they ask me I should help them to prepare the guys who had the arms.
And there the-- a rifle is-- or a gun, and this was-- this was a fortune of money.
But I started going from house to house in the beginning and to teach them how to handle.
Yeah.
And when-- and the-- when they had their arms, had to have it, I used to keep with my room, the bunker over there.
And when it came to go out, the people were afraid-- were afraid to take it.
They were afraid if they catch them.
You know?
I-- they-- where do you think you'll go, they'll catch you?
You go out, you go out.
Oh, this way or that way.
You have to-- only have to walk there.
Did you have a job when you were in the ghetto?
In the ghetto, yeah.
I was a plumber.
But it was not the work in the ghetto.
I had diploma and I had a-- assign, that I was allowed to go without anybody, by myself.
So I had a-- a-- a guy who was there, working before.
And he knew that he will help me if he'll take me to work over there.
And he took me.
He took a couple guys.
And we used to go out without anybody.
You know?
Any place you worked was [INAUDIBLE], you call it.
And you had to go together, like, 50 people, 100 people, to go to-- from-- from the ghetto to the work and came back together.
I had assign I could go without anything.
Because you have to fix someplace in-- in a German place, you didn't have to have any-- one man is enough.
So if you had this kind of a job, you can bring in.
You can buy. you can sell.
Forget about it.
Nobody got paid.
No--
I had to bring in something, or to sell something.
You can-- we-- the Germans didn't-- they were supposed to, maybe.
Maybe they paid something to the-- the ghetto government.
But-- but you couldn't see it.
You couldn't get paid.
But it wasn't too much, the pay was nothing.
The only money I needed when I came from the woods, I had to have a haircut.
I didn't have it for a year.
So I had to pay the barber.
Well, you know--
[LAUGHTER]
When I got in the ghetto, they took me to the-- to the-- to the partisans because they needed me.
I was a specialist in all this-- all this garbage.
So I was in the activities.

And when I became an instructor to handle arms, you know, a rifle and these things, a lot of the-- the guys who got killed over there, were wounded, they didn't know-- and-- and bullets was a big deal.
You-- you could-- you could have an arms, but you could have two bullets.
You couldn't buy no more.
What's two bullets?
See, it was very bad.
Then I became-- I became involved with a guy, Ring, not the [INAUDIBLE].
[INAUDIBLE] Ring, he was a-- a-- refugee.
And he-- he really was a very talented guy.
He-- and I know him before the ghetto.
And-- and-- and they ask for-- for policemen, you know, this-- they're working, the police.
So a lot of guys went there.
If you had experience, you know, like a soldier or something.
Then I say, it's not for me.
So I say, you stay and the police go [INAUDIBLE].
He became a-- a commissar, you know, one of the top.
And then was a guy, with an organization, it was politics.
One-- one organization was communist.
Because they could have connections through the outside.
They were communists.
They had connections.
Then it was the revolutionist.
And then they was from Mount Zion or some other [YIDDISH].
[LAUGHTER] And I didn't have it before.
And I didn't want then, to do it.
But this guy became-- he-- he was very talented, and-- and brave too.
He became a-- from nothing, he became a-- a commissar, they used to call it.
A precinct belonged to him, and he was the head of the precinct.
Maybe he-- when he was doing this kind of work, you cannot be a saint, you know?
You have to be a-- he knew me before.
So he ask me, he says, come to me.
And he was-- became the head of this organization, you know, I have-- and without me, he didn't even go, let me tell you the truth.
[LAUGHTER] Yeah.
In this way, it was we-- we came to the woods.
He was a big-- he was a-- a real good [INAUDIBLE].
I guess it was good.
But he wouldn't go without me.
We did.
I forgot it.
What was the name?
We was-- well, it was separate, you know, than we-- from the other ones.
Well, you know, the communist, we-- we were separate.
Because-- then the-- the-- border is-- guys from the-- the-- they rob the-- from-- from-- from Russia, the Lithuanian government, was a government over there.
You know?
And they used to send over a radio, guys, you know.
How did they send them?
They dropped them down from the top.
I knew these guys.
And that's the way it is.
And I took it easy.
I always slept good.
How did I leave the ghetto?
I-- I used to take them out.
Groups, I used to take out.
And I was the-- the-- the [NON-ENGLISH], he used to call it.
I would show them how to go.
Took them out from the city.
And I--
Through the outskirts.
And then over there, you-- you told them how to go.
And I-- I-- when I had to go out, I was from-- from-- from our group, I was from the last one.
But this guy, the commissar, he made his connections.
And he hired a guy who was the chief of-- of-- of-- of-- of the police, and Lithuanian.
We paid him for it.
We took it out, took us out.
But I was sitting near him.
This was-- he is-- he is-- I-- I should watch him, you know, the guy, this chief of police.
I watched him here and I-- and I-- I had the gun here, like this.
So he knew that we wouldn't go away if-- if he give us the gun.
And he brought us to a-- to the place that we used to call [INAUDIBLE].
And maybe give me a [INAUDIBLE].
I knew you didn't know what it it.
I didn't know either.
To stay in the woods?
I was-- soon as we were gone, they started making the-- the place over there.
I was there.
Yeah, yeah.
[POLISH].
And--
From the beginning.
Huh?
[LAUGHS] Anyway I get-- I was from the first and I was-- I-- I used to be the leader over there.
And now-- then I came back from [INAUDIBLE] some kind of work, you know, I had to-- to blow up the-- the-- the train, what do you call it?
Tracks.
This was my last-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] thing I had to do.
And I came back.
I came in the base where we were staying.
And nobody there.
[LAUGHS] We left for-- for Vilna.
But we left a guy, he was sick.
He couldn't go.
He told me where they went.
[BACKROUND NOISE] And I had a lot of people with me.
I had to send-- send them back to other places, where they belonged.
And I took my time.
I took it for-- I went over to Vilna.
But I was the last one.
So I closed up there.
Well-- well, I had trouble with other guys.
You mean, with other groups?
First of all, I had to do something.
And were were-- we got from-- the-- from the-- from the commanding Lithuanian government, what we have to blow up, or something like this.
Then we had to eat.
How did I get food?
We had to rob it.
Take it.
In the beginning they didn't want to give it to us.
Then they start.
I was ashamed to show my face to [?everybody?].
Like I-- I used to put on handkerchief.
I was ashamed, handkerchief on my face.
But this was in just the beginning.
Then I got-- I got used to it.
We had to do everything.
We didn't have a spoon and we didn't have a fork.
And we didn't have a plate.
And we didn't have no food.
And you have to get-- take it.
Do you know how you take a sheep from a place where they-- they-- the sheeps, you know, the sheeps.
The-- the-- first of all it's full of garbage.
You know, where the-- where we-- where we live, the place.
Then we have to catch them.
And they're so-- and then they have to kill 'em.
And then-- then we had to cut its throat.
I didn't do this business.
[LAUGHTER] But I used to bring them.
I used to bring them.
In the beginning we wouldn't take no-- no cows or-- or-- or oxes.
Because we didn't know how to handle them.
You have to-- you can't-- you cannot put them on the-- on the-- and bring them over like a sheep, you know?
But then if you had butchers and they became specialists.
They-- you know, they used to cut an ox.
They used to-- take him by his horn, like this.
And cut him here.
And he was-- when he was on-- on the ground, he was dead.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's the way it was.
And then they became specialists.
And sometimes you cut its throat and it got a-- away, a live one.
That's the way it is.
We became this-- you know this?
This was like if we were-- nobody knew nothing.
Nobody knew how to shoot.
Nobody knew how to handle something.
Nobody had no discipline, nothing.
And we had a lot of girls.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yeah.
And they were good, some of them.
Yes, sometimes.
But we couldn't take them a lot.
Because when-- when the-- the farmer see a girl come in, they lost respect.
They wouldn't give nothing.
But then we came out all right. then it was like-- like a you go to work.
In the beginning, all Jewish.
And later on, we had something-- some guys who escaped from-- from-- was it from Holland or for something like this?
And-- and you saw that he is-- he is-- he is a friend of the Jewish, we took him in.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was-- listen, well, they were there were more than a year.
They had to leave.
We didn't have no-- no-- no towel, we didn't have no soap.
We didn't have no hot water.
And we didn't have no cold water.
And who was the-- the-- the guy you-- you-- you [INAUDIBLE] in the book?
And who was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Who was that guy?
He [SOB].
And we had to build the bunkers.
You will see [INAUDIBLE].
Who built the-- built the bunkers?
Who built them?
All of a sudden, I became a-- a building technician.
You'll see what are [INAUDIBLE].
I build them.
And they're still standing.
Yeah.
No.
People went back and were in the-- when we were in the partisans, they say, so who's standing here that you want to see?
They say, the bunker.
They used to take-- to take them.
But I was doing it.
Nobody else was there.
[PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] I was the-- the commandeers who were there, you know, with Partisans, what rather than all the garbage-- and over me was a guy who was there.
But he wasn't from the party.
They You know, from the party, the-- the Communist party.
So they couldn't make me for use for the-- for the guy.
So he became the guy.
So I spoke to him a couple of months ago.
He said-- at least, he says, when--
[CRYING] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Brought back at least 300 people. 300 people that were our responsibility.
He says to me, so he said, at least we-- we did this.
If we wouldn't be there, they wouldn't be-- they wouldn't survive. 300 people.
[SIGHS]
The last-- the last thing it was, what I did, we had to blow up the-- the tracks.
And we had-- had over-- over 100 people.
And they used-- you have to lay it one after the other, you know?
They should blow up after the-- one after the other.
Because you have to take it a lot of-- it-- it was kilometers.
And so we did it.
And this was like, we blow it up.
And it was blow-- we were already on the base, and it was still blowing up.
And lot of people got killed in this business.
But not by me.
[LAUGHTER] I mean it.
And you know what?
They say, this guy is-- is lucky.
I am a lucky guy.
So everybody went to go with me.
It wasn't luck.
Well, the big army was the Russian, you know?
And the fighting thing, to blow up, you know, with-- we didn't have no-- should blow up the things.
You know, we didn't have no tanks.
We didn't have no-- but-- but they came-- we-- we came from one side and they came from the other side.
And then we took him-- I was the first one with the-- he went to the-- the Russian again.
And I took the place where-- where I told you, I tell you, the-- the seventh floor thing was there.
Because I knew it.
I took for us, [INAUDIBLE].
You know?
I-- The building with the elevator.
Yeah.
And there was this General over there.
A-- a-- a General.
He was from the-- from the-- Royal Air, the General from Lithuania.
So I said, you got a job for me?
I seemed authentic.
He gave me a job.
So you know what-- what's now you got a job in Russia.
You got a job.
But you don't have no pay.
So what they did for me, they gave me a-- I could eat in the-- in the-- in the fancy places where the-- the higher officers eat.
So they gave you a good dinner.
And you could buy cigarettes.
A couple packs, not too many.
And then they had to buy for this money, you had to buy on the market, you had to buy bread or something.
[LAUGHS]
Sure.
Because it came, the-- the Lithuanian government came.
And the Russian gave them this place.
It's your place, you know?
So they took it over, you know?
But they didn't mingle with us, what to do.
Or maybe sometimes you had to-- to do something together, you know?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
They gave us-- you saw there where they gave us a thing that said that this and this belong to this and this party.
And-- and they-- then they paid us back pay.
Well, which-- well, what do you mean, end of the war?
Everybody went for them self.
If you belonged to organization, you know, a Zionist organization, you went to-- to-- to-- to the HIAS.
I wented with a-- with a Zionist group, you know?
But I didn't belong to them.
I always was the leader, every place where I came.
Ah.
[CRYING] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was the leader.
When-- when they-- everybody needs somebody, something, they used to come to me.
But I did it.
No.
I wouldn't go for no place.
Well, I have to, you know, it's a cemetery.
No.
In the beginning, I think I was over there a little while.
And then I left.
You know, I left Poland.
Oh, that's a different story.
I left my mother and father in the big bunker.
Paid a lot of money to get in, in this, with an-- with an exit to the sewers.
And they figure if they catch the bunker, somebody is going to do this here, they'll go out to the sewers and go out.
Or well, what can you do?
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And that's what happened.
They were older people, you know.
They couldn't take it, what they have to.
Anyway, once I was going with-- with the-- with the group, you know, to-- to make something.
And the guy came from Vilna.
I saw right away he is a Jewish guy.
I say, was they in the ghetto?
He says, which one of them?
I say, over there and on this [INAUDIBLE], you know, this and-- it's a bunker.
You hear about it?
They're breaking, he says.
They're breaking the bunker to get in.
They cannot go in.
We made it the right way, you know, like a-- with a-- so I say, let's go back.
And this guy was with me, the guy he was, I told you, you know?
The-- the guy who-- who became a commissar, you know?
Ring.
He was with me because he-- his parents were in the bunker too.
He was a big shot in the ghetto.
So we went back.
We'll continue on the next tape.
